schools.summary: Paul turns to the 'collection' (logia, a gathering of money) for 'the saints' — the poor believers at Jerusalem, for whom Paul was raising relief among the Gentile churches (Romans 15:25-26); having already directed the Galatian churches in this matter, he bids the Corinthians follow the same order, a uniform apostolic arrangement (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): the relief of poor brethren is a sacred Christian duty; Paul organizes it carefully across the churches, that the Gentile believers might minister to the needy Jewish saints.

themes: concerning the collection for the saints
as I have given order to the churches of Galatia, even so do ye
the directions for the collection
organized relief of the poor saints

schools.summary: Each is to 'lay by' (set apart, at home or toward the common treasury) a portion 'in store,' regularly and proportionately — 'as God hath prospered him,' according to his means and gains — that the relief be ready in bulk when Paul comes, not hastily collected. The 'first day of the week' (the day after the Sabbath) is the day Christians met for worship; Barnes and Clarke take this as strong proof that the first day was observed as holy time, the Christian Sabbath, by apostolic authority (cf. Acts 20:7; Revelation 1:10).

Calvin: the day was chosen because they held sacred assemblies, and believers have always had a certain day of rest for worship; but (per Galatians 4:10) no day is to be distinguished with a view to religion as a service to God, only for external order — Christian liberty stands, the Lord's day chosen because the resurrection ended the shadows of the law; he also notes the giving is a treasure laid up with God, who repays the giver to the poor (Proverbs 19:17).

debates.question: Does 'upon the first day of the week' establish the Christian Sabbath / Lord's Day as holy time?

sides.position: Yes — strong proof that the first day was observed as the Christian Sabbath by apostolic authority
A day of sacred assembly, but no day is to be made a religious distinction; Christian liberty stands (Galatians 4:10)

themes: Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store
as God hath prospered him
systematic proportionate giving
the first day of the week

schools.summary: Paul's careful provision: when he comes, those whom the Corinthians themselves 'approve' (choose, accredit) 'by your letters' (with letters of commendation) he will send to carry their 'liberality' (their bountiful gift) to Jerusalem; leaving the choice of messengers to the church, that all may be done with transparency and confidence, avoiding any suspicion in the handling of the money (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: in matters of money, Paul is scrupulously careful to remove all ground for suspicion, entrusting the gift to men chosen and accredited by the donors themselves.

themes: whomsoever ye shall approve by your letters, them will I send
to bring your liberality unto Jerusalem
delivery by approved messengers
transparent stewardship of the gift

schools.summary: Paul adds that 'if it be meet' (suitable, fitting) — if the size of the gift or the circumstances warrant — that he himself should go too, then the chosen messengers shall go with him; he leaves it open whether he will personally accompany the collection, willing to do so if it seems best, but not insisting, the arrangement adapting to circumstances (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: Paul's whole concern is the safe and honourable delivery of the gift, ready to lend his own presence if that would serve the cause.

themes: if it be meet that I go also, they shall go with me
Paul's readiness to accompany the gift
willing to go if warranted
care for honourable delivery

schools.summary: Paul outlines his travel: he will come to Corinth after passing through Macedonia (the northern Greek province) — for he intends to make that journey, visiting the Macedonian churches on the way; a deliberate, settled plan, though (as 2 Corinthians shows) his movements were later somewhat changed by circumstances (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): Paul plans his journeys with a view to visiting and strengthening the churches; his travel is governed by his apostolic concern for them.

themes: I will come to you, when I shall pass through Macedonia
for I do pass through Macedonia
Paul's plan to come through Macedonia
travel governed by pastoral care

schools.summary: Paul expresses his hope: he may 'abide' (stay), even 'winter' with them — spend the winter season at Corinth — desiring a substantial visit, not a passing one; and anticipates that they will 'bring (set, escort) him on his journey,' help him forward, equip and accompany him as he continues — a token of fellowship and mutual partnership in the work (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: it may be that I will abide, yea, and winter with you
that ye may bring me on my journey
Paul's hope to winter at Corinth
extended fellowship and partnership

schools.summary: Paul does not wish merely to 'see them by the way' (in passing, a brief stop) but 'to tarry a while' — to make a real, unhurried visit, that the time might profit them; and he subordinates all his plans to the will of God: 'if the Lord permit' — he holds his intentions loosely, ready to defer to God's providence (cf. James 4:15, Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: even the apostle makes his plans conditionally, recognizing that all is governed by the Lord; a model of submitting our purposes to God.

themes: I will not see you now by the way; but I trust to tarry a while
if the Lord permit
a real visit submitted to God's will
plans held loosely under providence

schools.summary: Paul states his present plan: he will 'tarry (remain) at Ephesus until Pentecost' — staying in that city, where he was when writing, until the feast of Pentecost (in late spring), before setting out; the reference to Pentecost fixes the time of writing and shows Paul intended a further stay at Ephesus for the reason he gives next (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul does not leave a fruitful field hastily; he remains where the work prospers, governing his movements by the opportunities for the gospel.

themes: I will tarry at Ephesus until Pentecost
Paul's stay at Ephesus
remaining where the work prospers
the time of writing fixed

schools.summary: Paul's reason for staying: 'a great door and effectual (energes, productive) is opened' — a wide and promising opportunity for spreading the gospel at Ephesus, a field ripe for fruitful labour; and far from deterring him, the 'many adversaries' (opponents, enemies of the gospel) are part of why he stays — the very opposition marks the importance of the field and the need for his presence (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): an open door and strong opposition together call for steadfast labour, not retreat; Paul stays precisely because the work is great and contested.

themes: a great door and effectual is opened unto me
there are many adversaries
the open door and the many adversaries
steadfast labour in a contested field

schools.summary: Paul commends Timothy (his young fellow-worker) to the Corinthians: see that he be 'without fear' among them — that he be received kindly, not made anxious or discouraged by their factious or critical spirit; the reason for honouring him being that Timothy is engaged in the Lord's work, just as Paul is — a true minister, deserving the same respect, despite his youth (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): Paul protects his timid younger colleague, charging the church to treat him with the consideration due to one doing Christ's work.

themes: if Timotheus come, see that he may be with you without fear
for he worketh the work of the Lord, as I also do
the commendation of Timothy
kind reception of a true minister

schools.summary: Paul charges that 'no man despise him' — let none look down on Timothy for his youth or timidity (cf. 1 Timothy 4:12) — but rather 'conduct (escort, set) him forth in peace,' helping him on his way back to Paul, who awaits his return with the brethren and is concerned for his safe and honourable journey (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): youth and lowly appearance are no ground for contempt in Christ's servants; the church is to honour and assist faithful ministers regardless of age.

themes: Let no man therefore despise him: but conduct him forth in peace
for I look for him with the brethren
the charge not to despise Timothy
honour due to Christ's ministers

schools.summary: Paul 'greatly desired' (strongly urged) Apollos to go to Corinth with the brethren — showing no jealousy of Apollos (whom some Corinthians had set up as a rival, 1:12), but warmly wishing for his ministry; yet Apollos himself was unwilling to come just then, his own judgment (perhaps to avoid fostering the factions that bore his name) against it, and Paul respects his free choice (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul and Apollos are shown to be in full accord, Paul urging and Apollos declining for good reason — a rebuke to the Corinthian parties that had set them against each other.

themes: I greatly desired him to come unto you with the brethren
his will was not at all to come at this time
Paul's wish for Apollos and Apollos's choice
the concord of the apostles against the factions

schools.summary: Paul's stirring series of commands: 'watch ye' (be vigilant, alert, on guard against temptation, error, and the enemy); 'stand fast in the faith' (be firm, immovable in the truth of the gospel, not carried away by error — pointed given their wavering on the resurrection); 'quit you like men' (acquit yourselves as men, be manly, courageous); and 'be strong' (be vigorous, mighty) — a martial summons to manly courage and strength in the Christian warfare (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin (with Henry): these terse commands rouse the Corinthians, given to division and weakness, to vigilant, steadfast, courageous, and strong Christian living.

themes: Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong
the fourfold exhortation
watchfulness, steadfastness, courage, strength
a martial summons to spiritual manhood

schools.summary: Paul crowns the bracing commands of v.13 with the rule of love: 'let all your things be done with charity (love)' — all their conduct, including their courage and steadfastness, is to be governed and tempered by love; for manly courage and firmness, without love, would degenerate into harshness and faction (the very fault at Corinth), love being the indispensable atmosphere of all Christian action (Barnes).
Calvin: the strength and steadfastness just enjoined are not to breed contention; love must rule all, binding the whole life together — the summary of the epistle's great theme (ch. 13).

themes: Let all your things be done with charity
all to be done in love
love governing all conduct
courage tempered by love

schools.summary: Stephanas and his household were 'the firstfruits of Achaia' — among the first converts in that region (the Greek province), the earliest fruit of the gospel there, and so worthy of special regard; and they had 'addicted themselves' (devoted, given themselves up) to 'the ministry (service) of the saints,' voluntarily dedicating themselves to serving and helping fellow believers — a model of self-giving service (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: such devoted servants of the church deserve honour and recognition; Paul holds them up as examples and bespeaks for them the church's regard.

themes: the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia
they have addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints
the devoted household of Stephanas
the honour due to self-giving service

schools.summary: Paul's request (completing v.15): that the Corinthians 'submit themselves' (yield, defer, give due regard) to such devoted servants as Stephanas — and to 'every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth' — all who share in the work and toil of the gospel; those who give themselves to the church's service deserving the respect and willing cooperation of the rest (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: voluntary service in the church earns a real (though not lordly) claim to the deference of those served; faithful labourers are to be esteemed and supported.

themes: that ye submit yourselves unto such, and to every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth
the call to submit to devoted labourers
honour owed to faithful service
deference to the church's labourers

schools.summary: Paul rejoices at the coming of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus — three Corinthian believers who had come to him (perhaps bearing the church's letter and gifts), whose visit gladdened him; their presence 'supplied' what was lacking, making up by their personal coming for the absence of the whole church, bringing Paul the fellowship and information he could not have from the distant Corinthians (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: I am glad of the coming of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus
that which was lacking on your part they have supplied
Paul's gladness at the visit
the comfort of Christian fellowship

schools.summary: These brethren 'refreshed my spirit and yours' — their coming brought Paul comfort and rest of mind, and (by their report and return) would refresh the Corinthians too; mutual encouragement. Paul bids the church 'acknowledge' (recognize, honour, give due regard to) 'such' men — those who thus serve and refresh — that they be esteemed and welcomed (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: those who refresh the spirits of others in the Lord's work deserve grateful recognition; the church is to value such helpful and devoted members.

themes: they have refreshed my spirit and yours
acknowledge ye them that are such
the visitors refreshed Paul's spirit
grateful recognition of devoted servants

schools.summary: Paul conveys greetings from 'the churches of Asia' (the Roman province of Asia, around Ephesus where he was writing) — a token of the fellowship binding the churches; and the warm greeting from Aquila and Priscilla (Paul's tentmaker friends and fellow-workers, Acts 18) 'much in the Lord,' together with 'the church that is in their house' — a Christian congregation meeting in their home, a notable early instance of a house-church (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin (with Henry): these greetings express the love and communion of the saints across the churches; the house-church of Aquila and Priscilla shows the devotion of this godly couple.

themes: The churches of Asia salute you
Aquila and Priscilla salute you much in the Lord, with the church that is in their house
greetings from the Asian churches
the house-church of Aquila and Priscilla

schools.summary: Paul adds that 'all the brethren' (all the believers with him) greet the Corinthians — the universal love of the saints — and bids them 'greet one another with an holy kiss'; the 'holy kiss' was the customary token of Christian love and fellowship in the early church (Romans 16:16), a kiss of peace, 'holy' (chaste, pure, sacred), expressing brotherly affection and unity among believers (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the holy kiss signified reconciliation and mutual love; for the divided Corinthians especially, it was a call to put away strife and embrace one another in Christian affection.

themes: All the brethren greet you
Greet ye one another with an holy kiss
the greeting of all the brethren
the holy kiss of fellowship

schools.summary: Paul, who usually dictated his letters to an amanuensis (a scribe), here adds the closing salutation 'with mine own hand' — writing it personally as a mark of authenticity and affection; this autograph greeting was Paul's customary token (2 Thessalonians 3:17) by which his letters were known to be genuine, guarding against forgery, and it also added a personal warmth (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: The salutation of me Paul with mine own hand
Paul's authenticating greeting
the autograph token of genuineness
his personal seal

schools.summary: Paul makes love to Christ the decisive test of true Christianity, pronouncing whoever lacks it 'Anathema' (accursed, devoted to destruction) — not a vindictive wish but a declaration of what is true of, and ought to befall, the one who loves not Christ. 'Maranatha' is a separate Aramaic phrase, 'the Lord cometh' (or 'Our Lord, come'), wrongly joined to 'Anathema' by the King James Version (a 'curious blunder,' Robertson); a watchword of the Lord's coming, added as a solemn reminder that he will come to execute judgment (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: the anathema is against pretenders and hypocrites who take the Lord's name while not loving him from the heart, troubling the church for their own belly or empty boasting; 'let him be held by you as accursed' (as in Galatians 1:8), abhorred by believers — not necessarily condemned by God; Maranatha a customary Hebrew/Aramaic form in excommunication, 'the Lord cometh.'

Clarke (with Wesley): directed especially against the unbelieving Jews who called Jesus accursed (12:3) — a prediction, not an imprecation, of judgment that came upon them; 'Maranatha,' the Lord cometh, namely to execute vengeance, alluding to the curse that closes the Old Testament (Malachi 4:6).

debates.question: What does 'Maranatha' mean, and is it part of the curse?

sides.position: A separate Aramaic watchword, 'the Lord cometh' — wrongly joined to 'Anathema' by the KJV
'The Lord cometh' to execute vengeance — added to the anathema as a threat of judgment (esp. on the unbelieving Jews)

themes: If any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha
the solemn curse on those who love not Christ
love to Christ the decisive test
the Maranatha crux

schools.summary: Paul closes with his customary benediction: 'the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you' — invoking upon them the free favour, love, and saving help of Christ, the sum of every blessing; grace is ascribed to Christ as its source and channel, a mark of his divine dignity, the abiding favour of the Lord Jesus being the blessing Paul most desires for them (Barnes, Gill).
Cyril: after the solemn warning of v.22, Paul turns to bless those who do love Christ, that his grace may rest upon them — the gentle close balancing the severe sentence.

themes: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you
the apostolic benediction of grace
the grace of Christ invoked
the gentle close after the warning

schools.summary: Paul ends with a tender personal assurance: 'my love be with you all' — his own affection embraces them, the whole church, despite all his reproofs and corrections; love is his last word, the emphatic 'all' extending it even to those he had rebuked, the factious and erring, none excluded; his love 'in Christ Jesus,' a Christian love grounded in their common union with Christ, and 'Amen' seals the epistle (Barnes, Ellicott).

themes: My love be with you all in Christ Jesus. Amen
Paul's closing love to the whole church
love his last word
affection grounded in Christ
